g.data-narration: Welcome to Object-Oriented Programming! This module covers the fundamental concepts that form the backbone of modern software engineering.
OOP is a programming paradigm that organizes code around objects containing both data and behavior, revolutionizing software development.
g.text: A paradigm that uses "objects" - software entities with
data (attributes) and behavior (methods) - to model real-world entities.
Languages: C++, Java, Python, C#, Ruby | Evolved from Simula (1967) → Smalltalk (1970s) → C++/Java

g.data-narration: OOP provides better security through encapsulation, promotes code reuse via inheritance, and enables scalable software design.
g.text: Message Passing: Objects communicate via methods

g.data-narration: Objects are real-world entities with state and behavior. Classes serve as blueprints from which multiple objects can be created.
g.text: Objects and Classes: The Core Building Blocks

g.data-narration: Encapsulation bundles data and methods into a single unit, using access specifiers to control data visibility and ensure security.
Abstraction hides complex implementation details, showing only essential features. Abstract classes with pure virtual functions enable this.
Inheritance enables a derived class to acquire properties and behaviors from a base class, promoting code reusability.
g.text: Inheritance: Acquiring Properties from Parent Class
Single: one parent → one child | Multiple: multiple parents → one child | Multilevel: chain A→B→C

g.data-narration: There are five types of inheritance: single, multiple, multilevel, hierarchical, and hybrid. Each defines different parent-child relationships.
Compile-time polymorphism uses function overloading to create multiple functions with the same name but different parameters.
g.text: add(int a, int b) // int version
add(double a, double b) // double version

g.data-narration: Runtime polymorphism allows a base class pointer to call derived class methods dynamically, enabling flexible and extensible code.
Procedural programming focuses on procedures and algorithms, while OOP organizes code around objects with data and behavior combined.
g.text: Examples: C++, Java, Python vs C, Pascal

g.data-narration: The four pillars of OOP are encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction. These fundamental principles form the foundation of object-oriented design.
Remember these key exam points: class is blueprint, object is instance; private members are not accessible in derived classes; pure virtual functions create abstract classes.
Constructors initialize objects when created; destructors clean up when objects are destroyed. Constructor has same name as class, destructor has tilde prefix.
g.text: Constructor: ClassName() - same name, no return type, auto-called
Destructor: ~ClassName() - ~ prefix, auto-called on object destruction
